hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Rem publicam' — 'Res publica' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Re publica' — 'Res publica' is nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative would also be 'Res publica,' but a state is not being addressed here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'rei' ends in -ei
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'rei' ends in -ei
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'rei' ends in -ei

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'conservata est' translates to?

hints.to observe: 'To observe' is 'observare' — 'conservare' means to preserve or keep safe
hints.to conserve energy: While English 'conserve' has this sense, Latin 'conservare' means to keep safe, preserve the state
hints.to serve: 'To serve' is 'servire' — 'conservare' (con + servare) means to keep safe

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'conservata est'?

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'conservatur' — participle + 'est' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'conservata erat' — 'est' makes this perfect
hints.perfect active indicative: Perfect active would be 'conservavit' — participle + 'est' is perfect passive

hints.servo, servare, servavi, servatum: These are the parts of 'servare' (to save) — the base verb without the con- prefix
hints.servio, servire, servivi, servitum: These are the parts of 'servire' (to serve) — different verb entirely
hints.observo, observare, observavi, observatu: These are the parts of 'observare' (to observe) — different prefix

steps.prompt: Why does the participle 'conservata' end in -a?
choices: it is a first declension noun

hints.it is a neuter plural: Neuter plural nominative/accusative also ends in -a, but here it agrees with feminine 'Res'
hints.it is an ablative singular: Ablative of a 1st/2nd declension adjective ends in -a for feminine, but here it is nominative
hints.it is a first declension noun: 'Conservata' is a participle (verbal adjective), not a noun — it agrees with 'Res'

steps.prompt: What does 'publica' modify, and what case is it?

hints.ablative feminine singular: Ablative would be 'publica' too (1st/2nd decl. adj.), but here it matches nominative 'Res'
hints.nominative neuter plural: Neuter plural nominative would be 'publica,' but here it agrees with feminine 'Res'
hints.vocative feminine singular: Vocative would be the same form, but 'Res publica' is the subject, not being addressed

steps.prompt: This famous phrase closes Cicero's Third Catilinarian. What rhetorical effect does the passive voice achieve?
steps.target: emphasizes the republic as the beneficiary, not Cicero as agent
choices: emphasizes the republic as the beneficiary, not Cicero as agent
follows grammatical convention for state documents

hints.makes the sentence shorter: Brevity is a side effect — the passive foregrounds the republic's salvation, not the agent
hints.avoids naming the conspirators: The conspirators are not relevant here — the passive emphasizes what was saved
hints.follows grammatical convention for state: There is no such convention — Cicero's choice is rhetorical, centering 'Res publica'

hints.3rd declension adjective: 3rd declension adjectives have forms like -is, -e — 'publicus, -a, -um' is 1st/2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension is for nouns — 'publicus' is a 1st/2nd declension adjective
hints.5th declension: 5th declension is for nouns — 'publicus' is a 1st/2nd declension adjective

steps.prompt: In 'conservata est,' which word is the perfect passive participle and which is the auxiliary?
steps.target: 'conservata' is the participle, 'est' is the auxiliary
choices: 'conservata' is the participle, 'est' is the auxiliary
'est' is the participle, 'conservata' is the auxiliary
neither is a participle — it is one compound form

hints.'est' is the participle, 'conservata' is: 'Est' is a form of 'sum' (to be) used as auxiliary — 'conservata' is the participle
hints.both are participles: 'Est' is not a participle — it is a finite form of 'esse' serving as auxiliary
hints.neither is a participle — it is one comp: Latin perfect passive is analytically formed: participle (conservata) + auxiliary (est)
